subsystem: OpenLDAP slapd authorization-identity (authz) value normalizer. The function authzPrettyNormal() is the pretty/normalize handler for the authz rule syntax used by attributes such as authzTo/authzFrom and by SASL authorization mappings. It parses a single authz rule string in one of six forms (a bare <DN>; dn[.{exact|children|subtree|onelevel|regex}]:<DN>; u[.mech[/realm]]:<ID>; group[/groupClass[/memberAttr]]:<DN>; or an ldap:/// URL) and emits a canonical/normalized berval into *normalized.
provenance: Identified from OpenLDAP-specific identifiers: berval / BER_BVISNULL / BER_BVZERO / BER_BVNULL / BER_BVSTR / ber_bvccmp / ber_str2bv_x / ber_dupbv_x / ber_memalloc_x / ber_memfree_x (lber), LDAPURLDesc / ldap_url_parse / ldap_free_urldesc / ldap_url_desc2str (libldap), and slapd-internal symbols dnNormalize / dnPretty / slap_parse_user / slap_bv2ad / oc_bvfind / SLAP_LDAPDN_MAXLEN / LDAP_X_SCOPE_* / lutil_strcopy / lutil_strncopy / AC_MEMCPY / ch_free. The six-form comment block (lines 15-22) and function name authzPrettyNormal match the OpenLDAP slapd authz value pretty/normalize routine. The sample banner labels it primevul_c_train_3228_vuln.
trust_boundary: val (struct berval *) is the untrusted input: it carries an authorization-identity rule value that originates from an LDAP client (an authzTo/authzFrom attribute value, a SASL authzid, or an ACL by/peername authz clause). Both val->bv_val (the bytes) and val->bv_len (the declared length) are attacker-controlled and may be short, empty, embed NUL bytes, or not be NUL-terminated at bv_len. ctx is a caller-supplied memory context (often an operation slab / thread temp memctx). Everything the code reads out of *val — prefix bytes, offsets, and the derived sub-bervals passed to dnNormalize/str2filter/ldap_url_parse — is hostile.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function signature takes val (the untrusted authz rule to parse), normalized (an out-parameter berval that receives the canonical form), ctx (a memory context threaded into all allocations so the result is owned by the caller's arena), and normalize (a flag selecting dnNormalize vs dnPretty for DN canonicalization). Local bv is a working berval that is repeatedly re-pointed into val's buffer as parsing advances. rc defaults to LDAP_INVALID_SYNTAX so any un-handled path fails closed. ludp holds the parsed LDAP URL for form 6. lud_dn and lud_filter save the ORIGINAL ludp->lud_dn / ludp->lud_filter pointers so the cleanup block can distinguish them from replacement buffers. scope starts at -1 (invalid) and is set to an LDAP_X_SCOPE_* value once a DN scope keyword is recognized.

The only up-front validation is two asserts: val is non-NULL and BER_BVISNULL(val) is false (bv_val non-NULL / bv_len non-zero, depending on the macro's exact definition). Crucially these are assert() calls, which the C standard elides entirely when NDEBUG is defined (the normal production build), so in release builds there is effectively NO precondition check. BER_BVZERO(normalized) zeroes the output berval so callers see an empty result on any early-return error path. Note there is no check of val->bv_len against the minimum length the parser below will read.

The first branch handles the dn[...] forms. strncasecmp compares the first STRLENOF("dn")==2 bytes of val->bv_val against "dn" case-insensitively. On a match it declares local out (receives the normalized DN), prefix (the literal string prepended in the final result), and ptr (a write cursor). bv.bv_val is set to val->bv_val + 2, i.e. positioned just past the "dn" prefix so subsequent code can inspect the scope specifier. Only bv.bv_val is set here; bv.bv_len is computed later at the is_dn label.

This block decodes the optional scope selector. If the byte after "dn" is '.', it advances past it and matches one of the keyword+colon tokens ("exact:", "regex:", "children:", "subtree:", "onelevel:") with strncasecmp, advancing bv.bv_val past the matched token and setting scope accordingly; an unrecognized token returns LDAP_INVALID_SYNTAX. If the byte after "dn" is not '.', it must be ':' (the bare "dn:" form) which sets scope=EXACT and advances one byte; anything else is rejected. Each advance moves bv.bv_val further into val's buffer purely on the assumption that the matched literal bytes are actually present within val->bv_len.

strspn skips leading spaces. The is_dn: label is the convergence point for several fall-through paths (the group branch at line 201 and the URL BADSCHEME branch at line 318 both goto here). The key computation bv.bv_len = val->bv_len - (bv.bv_val - val->bv_val) derives the remaining DN length as the total declared length minus how far bv.bv_val has advanced from the start of val. bv_len here is an unsigned ber_len_t. If the derived DN is exactly the single character '*', ber_bvccmp returns true and the canonical result "dn:*" is written and the function returns success. Otherwise the DN is handed to the scope switch below.

For the four DN scopes (EXACT/CHILDREN/SUBTREE/ONELEVEL) the remaining bv is canonicalized by dnNormalize or dnPretty into out, using ctx for allocation; a non-success rc returns INVALID_SYNTAX. For REGEX the DN is NOT DN-normalized (a regex pattern is not a DN): the code sizes normalized->bv_len as strlen("dn.regex:") + bv.bv_len, allocates bv_len+1 bytes with ber_memalloc_x(ctx), copies the literal "dn.regex:" then lutil_strncopy's bv.bv_len bytes from bv.bv_val, and NUL-terminates. The correctness of this allocation and copy depends entirely on bv.bv_len being a correct, non-underflowed length. The default case rejects any unset/invalid scope.

For the four DN scopes, a literal prefix string is chosen via BER_BVSTR (which sets prefix.bv_val/bv_len to the literal and its strlen). The final buffer is sized as prefix.bv_len + out.bv_len + 1, allocated with ctx, then assembled by copying the prefix and the normalized DN out and NUL-terminating. Importantly, out.bv_val (the dnNormalize/dnPretty result, itself ctx-allocated) is released here with ber_memfree_x(out.bv_val, ctx) — the correct ctx-matched deallocator. This establishes the intended contract: ctx-allocated buffers are freed with ber_memfree_x(...,ctx).

The second branch handles the u[.mech[/realm]]:<ID> form. It reads val->bv_val[0] and val->bv_val[1] directly to detect a leading 'u'/'U' followed by ':', '/', or '.'. It then copies the whole value into a fixed on-stack buffer buf[SLAP_LDAPDN_MAXLEN]. This branch is length-safe: it explicitly rejects val->bv_len >= sizeof(buf) BEFORE the strncpy, and the strncpy is bounded by sizeof(buf), so no stack overflow occurs. slap_parse_user splits the copied id into user/realm/mech; on success the original val is duplicated verbatim into normalized (line 174) and returned.

The third branch handles the group[/groupClass[/memberAttr]]:<DN> form when the value begins with "group". It re-points bv past the "group" prefix and directly computes bv.bv_len = val->bv_len - STRLENOF("group") (subtracting 5, unsigned). ber_bvchr searches bv for the ':' that separates the group spec from the DN; if none is found, the code falls back to treating the whole value as an exact DN (goto is_dn). group_dn.bv_val now points at the ':'.

If the character immediately after "group" is '/', an optional /groupClass[/memberAttr] spec is parsed by pointer arithmetic between the '/' positions and the ':' at group_dn.bv_val. group_oc spans from just after '/' to the ':'; an inner '/' further splits off member_at. member_at is resolved by slap_bv2ad and, on success, REPLACED with the schema canonical name ad->ad_cname (a different pointer/length owned by the schema, not by val). group_oc is likewise looked up with oc_bvfind and replaced by oc->soc_cname. Finally group_dn.bv_val is advanced past ':' and group_dn.bv_len is recomputed as the tail length via another unsigned subtraction from val->bv_len.

The group DN is canonicalized into out. The output is sized as strlen("group:") + out.bv_len, plus "/"+group_oc.bv_len and optionally "/"+member_at.bv_len when those were set. The final buffer is allocated with ctx and assembled with the same length fields that were summed, so the size accounting is internally consistent (group_oc/member_at were already replaced with schema-owned canonical names of matching length). out.bv_val is released with the correct ber_memfree_x(...,ctx). This branch's buffer assembly is self-consistent.

If none of the dn/u/group prefixes matched, the value is treated as an ldap:/// URL and parsed with ldap_url_parse into ludp. On success the scheme must be exactly "ldap" (case-insensitive); otherwise it errors to the done cleanup. AC_MEMCPY then lowercases the scheme in place by copying the 4 literal bytes "ldap" over the existing (already-confirmed "ldap") scheme buffer — safe because the destination already held a 4-char scheme. A BADSCHEME result frees ludp and falls back to exact-DN handling via is_dn. Any other error goes to done.

The URL must be of the form ldap:///... : host, attrs and extensions must all be empty, else error. The filter component, if present, is validated by round-tripping it through str2filter (parse) and filter2bv (re-serialize). The ORIGINAL ludp->lud_filter pointer is saved in lud_filter, and ludp->lud_filter is then REPLACED with the normalized filterstr.bv_val. filter2bv/filterstr are allocated by the default lber allocator (no ctx), which matters for how they must later be freed.

The URL's searchbase (lud_dn) is mandatory. The ORIGINAL ludp->lud_dn is saved in lud_dn, converted to a berval, and DN-canonicalized into a local out via dnNormalize/dnPretty using ctx — so out.bv_val is a CTX-ALLOCATED buffer. ludp->lud_dn is then REPLACED with out.bv_val. After zeroing the port, ldap_url_desc2str re-serializes the (now-normalized) URL descriptor into normalized->bv_val, and its length is measured. Execution then falls through into the done: cleanup regardless of success.

The cleanup restores ludp to its original allocation state so ldap_free_urldesc can free it with its own (libldap) allocator. For the filter: if ludp->lud_filter was replaced (!= saved lud_filter), the replacement (filterstr.bv_val, default-lber-allocated) is freed with ber_memfree, then the original is restored. For the DN: if ludp->lud_dn was replaced (!= saved lud_dn), the replacement is freed with ch_free and the original restored. The replacement DN, however, is out.bv_val from dnNormalize/dnPretty, which was allocated with ber_memalloc_x(...,ctx) — so freeing it with ch_free (a non-ctx free) mismatches the allocator. Finally ldap_free_urldesc frees the whole (restored) descriptor.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Mismatched deallocator (CWE-763). On the URL path, ludp->lud_dn is replaced at line 373 with out.bv_val, which dnNormalize/dnPretty allocated via ber_memalloc_x(size, ctx) (line 364/366). When ctx is a real per-operation slab / thread memory context (the common slapd case, e.g. op->o_tmpmemctx), that memory MUST be released with ber_memfree_x(ptr, ctx); the ctx allocator does not use libc malloc. Here it is freed with ch_free (the plain slapd malloc-wrapper free, i.e. ber_memfree_x(ptr, NULL)). Freeing a slab/ctx-owned pointer through the wrong allocator corrupts the slab bookkeeping (or hits a slab assertion / SIGSEGV). Contrast the filter cleanup two lines up, which correctly matches filterstr's default-lber allocation with ber_memfree, and the DN branches at lines 143/275 which correctly free their ctx buffers with ber_memfree_x(...,ctx). The invariant: a buffer allocated with ber_memalloc_x(...,ctx) must be freed with ber_memfree_x(...,ctx). An attacker who supplies a well-formed ldap:///<dn> value that normalizes successfully drives this free on every request; on a slab ctx it is remotely triggerable heap/slab corruption.

Unsigned length underflow (CWE-191). bv.bv_len is an unsigned ber_len_t. bv.bv_val has been advanced by the prefix/scope parsing (and by strspn over spaces) purely by matching literal bytes; the arithmetic assumes bv.bv_val <= val->bv_val + val->bv_len. If val->bv_len UNDER-reports the bytes actually consumed — e.g. the berval is not NUL-terminated at bv_len, or bv_len is set shorter than the literal tokens that strncasecmp matched against the underlying buffer — then (bv.bv_val - val->bv_val) exceeds val->bv_len and the subtraction wraps to a value near SIZE_MAX. That gigantic bv.bv_len then flows into dnNormalize/dnPretty and, on the REGEX path, into ber_memalloc_x(normalized->bv_len + 1) and lutil_strncopy(ptr, bv.bv_val, bv.bv_len) at lines 103-106, producing a massive over-read of val's buffer and heap overflow of the destination. The implementation must reject inputs where the consumed prefix length exceeds val->bv_len BEFORE computing the remainder, and must not treat val as NUL-terminated independent of bv_len.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
bv.bv_val = val->bv_val + STRLENOF( "group" );
		bv.bv_len = val->bv_len - STRLENOF( "group" );
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Second unsigned-underflow site (CWE-191), distinct from the is_dn one because the subtraction here is against a fixed constant. The branch is entered when strncasecmp(val->bv_val, "group", 5)==0. strncasecmp stops at the first NUL, so a value whose buffer begins "group" but whose declared val->bv_len is less than 5 (e.g. bv_len==2 while the backing buffer happens to contain "group...") passes the compare, and val->bv_len - STRLENOF("group") underflows to ~SIZE_MAX. bv is then handed to ber_bvchr(&bv, ':') which will scan up to that wrapped length looking for ':', reading far past the end of val's buffer (CWE-125 over-read), and any subsequent group_dn.bv_len at line 240 inherits the corruption. The implementation must verify val->bv_len >= STRLENOF("group") before subtracting, rather than relying on the NUL-terminated compare.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
} else if ( ( val->bv_val[ 0 ] == 'u' || val->bv_val[ 0 ] == 'U' )
			&& ( val->bv_val[ 1 ] == ':' 
				|| val->bv_val[ 1 ] == '/' 
				|| val->bv_val[ 1 ] == '.' ) )
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Out-of-bounds read / missing input-length validation (CWE-125 / CWE-20). The function never checks val->bv_len before dereferencing fixed byte offsets. Here it reads val->bv_val[0] and val->bv_val[1] unconditionally. A one-byte value "u" or "U" (bv_len==1) that is not NUL-terminated at bv_len makes the val->bv_val[1] read fall past the end of the buffer. The same pattern appears at line 34 (strncasecmp of 2 bytes), line 41/69 (bv.bv_val[0] after advancing past "dn"), and the scope-keyword strncasecmp calls at lines 44-60, all of which read literal-length runs from val without first confirming those bytes are within val->bv_len. The two asserts at lines 24-25 do not help: they are compiled out under NDEBUG and in any case only check non-NULL/non-empty, not a minimum length. The implementation must bound every byte access on val->bv_len and must not assume the berval is NUL-terminated.

Heap over-read / potential overflow downstream of the length underflow (CWE-125/CWE-787). This is the concrete consumer of the possibly-underflowed bv.bv_len from line 79 on the REGEX scope. If bv.bv_len wrapped huge, normalized->bv_len + 1 wraps on the add (CWE-190) and the allocation is tiny, while lutil_strncopy copies bv.bv_len bytes from bv.bv_val — a large over-read of val and an overflow of the undersized normalized->bv_val. Even absent underflow, this path trusts bv.bv_len to exactly equal the number of readable bytes at bv.bv_val; the safety of the copy is entirely a function of the parser arithmetic upstream, with no independent bound against the end of val's buffer. The implementation must guarantee bv.bv_len is a valid, non-wrapped count of readable bytes before this allocate-and-copy.

summary: authzPrettyNormal() parses an untrusted LDAP authorization-identity value into six sub-grammars. The headline defect is a mismatched-allocator free (CWE-763): on the ldap:/// path the searchbase DN is normalized into ctx-allocated memory (ber_memalloc_x(...,ctx)) but the cleanup releases it with ch_free instead of ber_memfree_x(...,ctx), corrupting a slab/temp memory context on a remotely reachable path. Secondary risks are unsigned length underflows when deriving DN sub-lengths (CWE-191, at the is_dn label and the group branch) and unbounded byte reads that trust NUL-termination instead of val->bv_len (CWE-125/CWE-20). The u-branch stack copy is correctly bounded.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: authzPrettyNormal(struct berval *val, struct berval *normalized, void *ctx, int normalize)
reviewed_anchors.reason: entry point; val is fully attacker-controlled and is read without a bv_len precondition (see CWE-125/CWE-20 finding)
hostile; bv_val bytes and bv_len drive all parsing, indexing and length arithmetic
out-parameter zeroed at line 26 and only written via sized allocations; no direct contract violation by itself
buffers allocated with ber_memalloc_x(...,ctx) must be freed with ber_memfree_x(...,ctx); the done: DN free uses ch_free (CWE-763)
boolean selector between dnNormalize/dnPretty; no memory-safety contract
bv.bv_len computed by unsigned subtraction at lines 79 and 195 can underflow (CWE-191)
saved-original vs replacement pointer dance in the done: block; DN replacement freed with wrong allocator (CWE-763)
scope is validated by the switch default cases; unset scope fails closed to LDAP_INVALID_SYNTAX
unsigned underflow when consumed prefix exceeds val->bv_len (CWE-191)
unsigned underflow when val->bv_len < 5 but the NUL-terminated compare matched (CWE-191)
fixed-offset reads with no bv_len bound; over-read on short/non-terminated bervals (CWE-125/CWE-20)
consumes possibly-underflowed bv.bv_len into ber_memalloc_x + lutil_strncopy; over-read/overflow (CWE-125/787/190)
length-safe: rejects sizeof(buf) <= val->bv_len before a strncpy bounded by sizeof(buf)
compiled out under NDEBUG and only checks non-null/non-empty; noted as inadequate validation but not itself a memory bug
destination already confirmed to be a 4-char "ldap" scheme at line 298; in-place 4-byte overwrite is safe

reviewed_anchors.anchor: group_oc/member_at replaced by schema names + normalized assembly (lines 227-274)
reviewed_anchors.reason: size accounting at 251-256 uses the same bv_len fields as the writes at 260-273; internally consistent
filterstr is default-lber-allocated and correctly freed with ber_memfree; allocator matches
correct ctx-matched free of dnNormalize/dnPretty output; establishes the intended contract the done: DN free violates
used only to size buf and gate the length check; no sign/overflow issue in shown use
libldap-owned allocation measured with strlen; ownership transferred to caller, no unsafe arithmetic here

findings.title: ctx-allocated URL searchbase DN freed with wrong allocator (ch_free) in cleanup
findings.bug_class: Release of memory via mismatched allocator (invalid free)
findings.invariant: Memory obtained from ber_memalloc_x(size, ctx) — here out.bv_val produced by dnNormalize/dnPretty at lines 364/366 and assigned to ludp->lud_dn at line 373 — MUST be released with ber_memfree_x(ptr, ctx) using the SAME ctx, never with ch_free/free.
findings.failure_mode: On the ldap:///<dn> path, ludp->lud_dn is replaced by the ctx-allocated normalized DN. In the done: cleanup, because that replacement differs from the saved original lud_dn, it is freed with ch_free (ber_memfree_x(ptr, NULL) / libc free). When the caller's ctx is a slab or per-thread temp memory context (the typical slapd operation context), freeing a slab-owned pointer through the plain allocator corrupts slab metadata or trips a slab assertion, i.e. remote heap/slab corruption. An attacker triggers it with any ldap:///<dn> authz value whose DN normalizes successfully. The sibling filter cleanup one branch above correctly uses ber_memfree to match filterstr's default-lber allocation, and the dn/group branches free their ctx buffers with ber_memfree_x(...,ctx) at lines 143/275 — this DN free is the lone allocator mismatch.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm dnNormalize/dnPretty allocate out.bv_val from ctx, that ch_free resolves to a non-ctx free (ber_memfree_x(...,NULL)), and whether call sites pass a non-NULL slab ctx; the fix is to free ludp->lud_dn with ber_memfree_x(ludp->lud_dn, ctx) when it was replaced by the normalized DN.

findings.title: Unsigned underflow when deriving remaining DN length (is_dn) drives huge copy/normalize
findings.bug_class: Integer underflow producing oversized length (heap over-read / overflow)
findings.invariant: bv.bv_val must always satisfy (bv.bv_val - val->bv_val) <= val->bv_len; the code must reject inputs where prefix/scope parsing consumed more bytes than val->bv_len before computing the unsigned remainder.
findings.failure_mode: bv.bv_len is unsigned. bv.bv_val is advanced only by matching literal bytes against the backing buffer (strncasecmp on "dn", scope keywords, strspn over spaces), never re-validated against val->bv_len. If val->bv_len under-reports the consumed bytes (non-NUL-terminated berval, or bv_len shorter than the matched literals), (bv.bv_val - val->bv_val) exceeds val->bv_len and the subtraction wraps to ~SIZE_MAX. That value flows into dnNormalize/dnPretty and, for scope REGEX, into ber_memalloc_x and lutil_strncopy at lines 103-106, yielding a massive over-read of val and heap overflow of the destination.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether callers guarantee val is NUL-terminated exactly at val->bv_len, and add a guard that bv.bv_val does not advance past val->bv_val + val->bv_len before line 79 subtracts.

findings.title: Unsigned underflow in group branch: val->bv_len - STRLENOF("group")
findings.code:
bv.bv_val = val->bv_val + STRLENOF( "group" );
		bv.bv_len = val->bv_len - STRLENOF( "group" );
findings.bug_class: Integer underflow producing oversized length (out-of-bounds read)
findings.invariant: The group branch must verify val->bv_len >= STRLENOF("group") before subtracting, rather than inferring length from the NUL-terminated strncasecmp match.
findings.failure_mode: The branch is gated by strncasecmp(val->bv_val, "group", 5)==0, which stops at the first NUL and therefore matches even if val->bv_len < 5 while the backing buffer contains "group...". val->bv_len - STRLENOF("group") then underflows to ~SIZE_MAX and is stored in bv.bv_len. ber_bvchr(&bv, ':') at line 196 scans up to that wrapped length searching for ':', reading far past the end of val's buffer (CWE-125), and any group_dn.bv_len derived downstream is corrupted.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the minimum val->bv_len enforced by callers for authz values and add an explicit val->bv_len >= STRLENOF("group") check before the subtraction at line 195.

findings.title: Fixed-offset byte reads of val without a bv_len bound (assumes NUL-termination)
findings.code:
} else if ( ( val->bv_val[ 0 ] == 'u' || val->bv_val[ 0 ] == 'U' )
			&& ( val->bv_val[ 1 ] == ':' 
				|| val->bv_val[ 1 ] == '/' 
				|| val->bv_val[ 1 ] == '.' ) )
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read from missing length validation
findings.invariant: Every read of val->bv_val at a fixed offset (indices 0/1 here; the 2-byte and keyword strncasecmp compares at lines 34,44-60; bv.bv_val[0] at lines 41,69) must be bounded by val->bv_len; the code must not assume val is NUL-terminated at bv_len.
findings.failure_mode: The function only asserts non-NULL/non-empty (lines 24-25, elided under NDEBUG) and never checks val->bv_len. A value of length 1 ("u"/"U") that is not NUL-terminated at bv_len makes the val->bv_val[1] read cross the buffer end; likewise a length-0 or length-1 value reaches the strncasecmp at line 34 which reads up to 2 bytes. Impact is a small out-of-bounds read (information disclosure or crash), gated by whether the backing allocation happens to be NUL-terminated.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether the authz syntax layer guarantees NUL-terminated, non-empty bervals; if not, add val->bv_len minimum-length guards before the indexed reads and prefix comparisons.

audit_checklist: Confirm ludp->lud_dn (the ctx-allocated normalized DN from line 373) is freed with ber_memfree_x(..., ctx) rather than ch_free at line 398, and that no slab/temp ctx is ever freed through ch_free/free anywhere in the function.
Verify dnNormalize/dnPretty allocate their out.bv_val from the passed ctx, and audit every free site (lines 143, 275, 391, 398) for allocator/ctx symmetry.
Add and verify a val->bv_len minimum-length precondition (and do not rely on NDEBUG-elided asserts) before any val->bv_val[...] index or multi-byte strncasecmp at lines 34, 44-60, 69, 150-153.
Guarantee bv.bv_val never advances past val->bv_val + val->bv_len so the unsigned subtraction at line 79 cannot wrap; add a reject-if-consumed>bv_len guard.
Add an explicit val->bv_len >= STRLENOF("group") check before line 195 so the group-branch subtraction cannot underflow when the NUL-terminated compare matches a short berval.
Trace the REGEX path (lines 103-107): confirm bv.bv_len is a validated in-bounds count before ber_memalloc_x(normalized->bv_len + 1) and lutil_strncopy, and that normalized->bv_len + 1 cannot wrap.
Confirm ldap_url_desc2str output ownership and that normalized->bv_val is released by the caller with the matching allocator on all return paths.
